one_line_takeaway: By forcing an LLM agent to echo a gradient-optimized adversarial string across its own Chain-of-Thought and tool calls, an attacker can turn the agent into a proxy that simultaneously fools itself and the CoT/action monitor watching it -- and hybrid (CoT+action) monitors, though stronger against static injections, are shown to be paradoxically more exploitable this way than CoT-only monitors, with no model-scale advantage protecting even 72B-parameter monitors.
key_finding: GCG/Parallel-GCG-optimized injection strings raise ASR far above the static AgentDojo baseline across all three monitor protocols (Table 2): AlignmentCheck ASR@5 rises from a 0.0-14.4% static baseline to 42.3% (Llama-3.1-8B), 93.3% (Mistral-7B), 90.7% (Qwen2.5-7B); PromptGuard2+AlignmentCheck rises to 76.3/99.0/86.6% respectively; extract-and-evaluate rises to 54.7/95.5/37.2%. Hybrid (CoT+action) monitoring, while more effective than CoT-only monitoring against static baseline attacks, is paradoxically *more* vulnerable to the adaptive attack than CoT-only monitoring (Table 4: e.g. Qwen2.5-7B hybrid static ASR 0.0% vs CoT-only static 41.6%, but hybrid adaptive ASR 68.2% vs CoT-only adaptive 55.1%) -- attributed to the added tool-call channel giving the attacker an extra surface on which to repeat the adversarial string. A capability gap is not required to defeat a monitor: GPT-4o-mini (LMArena score 1318) and Llama-3.1-70B (score 1294) -- models of comparable or lower capability than the Qwen2.5-72B monitor (score 1303) -- achieve 46.0% and 60.2% ASR@5 respectively against it (Table 5), up from a 2.3% static baseline. Parallel-GCG outperforms vanilla single-point GCG, especially where GCG's baseline ASR is low (Figure 4, e.g. Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct: 0.45->0.57, c4ai-command-r7b: 0.89->0.91, Qwen3-8B: 0.57->0.58). Attack strings transfer across model families/sizes up to 405B parameters (Table 6, Appendix C) with limited dependence on optimization-set size k. End-to-end live agent execution shows a modest 5-8 percentage point ASR drop versus simulated placeholder substitution (Table 3), attributed to imperfect verbatim reproduction of high-perplexity strings by the agent. A secondary finding: the extract-and-evaluate monitor's high Llama-3.1-8B ASR (54.7%@5) partly stems from a score-parsing bug (unparseable outputs default to score 0) rather than genuine semantic deception.
core_contribution: The paper introduces the 'Agent-as-a-Proxy' attack: an indirect prompt injection (IPI) strategy that treats the victim LLM agent as a delivery mechanism for an adversarial string, coercing the agent to repeat an optimized token sequence verbatim inside its own Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and/or tool calls so that a downstream monitoring LLM (which reads that CoT/tool-call trace to judge alignment with user intent) is itself deceived into labeling a malicious trajectory as safe. To generate strings that survive being echoed at multiple, non-identical insertion points in the trace (reasoning text, function arguments, execution logs), the authors propose Parallel-GCG, a modification of Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) that sums the coordinate gradient across K simultaneous insertion sites (derived via a captured reference trace S(a) = c0 (+) a (+) c1 (+) a ... (+) cK) rather than optimizing a single static context. For the LlamaFirewall defense (PromptGuard2 + AlignmentCheck), they instead use multi-objective GCG (from prior work, Zhan et al. 2025) with loss L_joint = alpha*L_alignmentcheck + (1-alpha)*L_pg2, alpha=0.5, to simultaneously fool the input-filter classifier and the hybrid monitor.
threat_model: White-box, gradient-based adaptive attacker who has full access to the monitoring LLM's weights/logits (GCG requires gradients) but does not need the agent itself to be stronger, aware of, or complicit in attacking the monitor -- the agent is merely a 'vessel' that echoes the adversarial string. The attacker embeds the optimized string inside an indirect prompt injection payload (e.g., injected into a Slack message or webpage) delivered via AgentDojo's 'important instructions' template. The target is monitoring-based AI control protocols that jointly observe agent CoT and tool-call actions (hybrid monitors such as AlignmentCheck/LlamaFirewall) or a two-stage extract-and-evaluate monitor, evaluated against both white-box open-weight monitor models (Llama-3.1-8B, Mistral-7B-v0.3, Qwen2.5-7B, and up to Qwen2.5-72B) and cross-model transfer up to Llama-3.1-405B. The paper explicitly notes in its Limitations section that it only attacks white-box monitors and that resulting attack strings have high perplexity (potentially detectable by perplexity filters or humans).
limitations: Self-reported by the authors (Section 7): (1) attack strings have high perplexity and could plausibly be detected by perplexity-based filters or human review -- the paper does not test against such filters; (2) the attack requires white-box access (gradients) to the monitoring model and has not been extended to black-box monitors, though the authors suggest PAL (Sitawarin et al., 2024) as a plausible extension; (3) Parallel-GCG's optimization objective assumes the agent trajectory is fixed and that the attack string is echoed verbatim at every site, which is an approximation -- in practice the agent's future trajectory can depend on the attack string and the LLM may not reproduce it exactly (acknowledged as the source of the E2E performance drop in Table 3). Not explicitly discussed but evident from the paper: experiments are confined to two AgentDojo suites (Slack, Banking) and one injection template ('important instructions'); the extract-and-evaluate ASR figures conflate genuine monitor deception with a score-parsing implementation bug specific to Llama-3.1-8B.

datasets_used: AgentDojo v1.2.1 (Debenedetti et al., 2024) — Slack suite (20 user tasks x 5 injection tasks) and Banking suite (15 user tasks x 8 injection tasks); 'important instructions' injection attack template only
quantitative_results: AlignmentCheck ASR@5: baseline->optimized: Llama-3.1-8B 0.0%->42.3%, Mistral-7B 14.4%->93.3%, Qwen2.5-7B 2.1%->90.7% (Table 2)

Hybrid vs CoT-only monitoring paradox (Table 4, ASR%): Qwen2.5-7B static CoT 41.6% vs static hybrid 0.0%, but adaptive CoT 55.1% vs adaptive hybrid 68.2%; Llama-3.1-8B adaptive CoT 65.2% vs adaptive hybrid 69.3%; Mistral-7B v0.3 adaptive CoT 88.8% vs adaptive hybrid 98.9%
Proxy-agent attack on Qwen2.5-72B monitor (Table 5): GPT-4o-mini baseline ASR 2.3% -> k=1 ASR 26.7%, ASR@5 46.0%; Llama-3.1-70B baseline ASR 2.3% -> k=1 ASR 19.4%, ASR@5 60.2%

Parallel-GCG vs GCG ASR (Figure 4): Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct 0.45->0.57, Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3 0.85->0.84 (no gain), Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct 0.41->0.54, c4ai-command-r7b-12-2024 0.89->0.91, Qwen3-8B 0.57->0.58

Simulated vs end-to-end execution ASR (Table 3): Mistral-7B v0.3 static 12.5% / simulated 57.5% / E2E 50.0%; Qwen2.5-7B static 0.0% / simulated 20.6% / E2E 17.5%; Llama-3.1-8B static 36.1% / simulated 66.0% / E2E 54.6%

Transferability up to 405B (Table 6, selected): Llama-3.1-8B-optimized strings vs llama-3.1-405b-instruct target: base 0.0%, k=1 88.2%, ASR@5 95.5%; vs Qwen-2.5-72B-Instruct target: base 0.0%, k=1 40.7%, ASR@5 54.5%
baselines_compared: Static/unmodified AgentDojo prompt injection attacks (per-monitor, per-model baseline ASR in Table 2/3/4/5/6)
Vanilla single-point GCG (Zou et al., 2023) as baseline for Parallel-GCG (Figure 4)
AlignmentCheck / hybrid monitor vs. modified CoT-only monitor (ablation, Table 4)
Simulated (placeholder substitution in pre-recorded trace) vs. full end-to-end live agent execution (Table 3)
Replay (step-by-step) vs. no-replay (full-trace-at-once) monitor evaluation modes (Figure 3)
